text: The mechanical behavior of two vacuum-melted heats of Hastelloy N was evaluated at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The material was subjected to several thermal-mechanical treatments and then irradiated at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal dose of $2.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
These observations are explained on the basis of helium production in the metal by the $^{10}\mathrm{B}(n,\alpha)$ transmutation.
Previous studies at ORNL<sup>1,2</sup> have shown that the high-temperature mechanical properties of this alloy deteriorate under neutron irradiation.
However, these studies involved air-melted material with B levels in the range of 20 to 50 ppm.
We have recently run two series of experiments that were aimed primarily at characterizing this alloy for use in the SNAP-8 system.
This system will utilize thin-walled Hastelloy N tubing for fuel element cladding and will operate over the temperature range of 650 to $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

One series of experiments involved in-reactor tube-burst tests on cladding material, and the details of this study have been reported. $^3$ The second series of experiments involved postirradiation creep rupture and tensile tests on small bar samples, and these results are presented in this report.
The two lots of material used in this study were 12-in.-diam, 10,000-lb double vacuum-melted heats obtained from Allvac Metals Company.
The chemical analysis of each heat is given in Table 1.
Heat 5911 was obtained in two forms: forged to a bar $2 \times 2$ in.
(designated 5911 AW) and forged and machined to a tube shell 2-in.
Heat 6252 was obtained in the as-cast condition (designated 6252 AC).
These treatments are described in Table 2 and will be referred to by number.

1 was used for in- and ex-reactor tests.
The specimens were irradiated in a single-test capsule in the P-4 poolside position in the ORR.
The peak thermal flux was $6 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2} \sec^{-1}$ , and the peak fast ( $>2.9 \mathrm{Mev}$ ) flux was $5 \times 10^{12}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2} \sec^{-1}$ .
The duration of the experiment was 1080 hr

(time at temperature and full power), so the thermal and fast doses were $2.3 \times 10^{20}$ and $1.9 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ , respectively.
Some of the specimens were controlled at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but most of them were held at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The environment in the capsule was flowing He-1% O $_2$ .
The temperature accuracy was $\pm 0.75\%$ , the guaranteed accuracy of the Chromel-P-Alumel thermocouples used.
The extensometer (mechanical and electrical portions) produced measurements that could be read to about $\pm 0.02\%$ strain.
However, other factors (temperature changes in the cell, mechanical vibrations, etc.) probably combined to give an overall accuracy of $\pm 0.1\%$ strain.
This error and the thermocouple accuracy combine to give a temperature uncertainty of about $\pm 1\%$ .
The results of tensile tests run on the materials in this study are summarized in Tables 3 and 4.
All of the unirradiated specimens were subjected to a thermal aging treatment (1080 hr at 650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) equivalent to that of the irradiated specimens.
The data in Table 3 indicate several important features of the tensile properties of the unirradiated materials.
The yield stress decreases slightly with increasing test temperature, whereas the tensile stress decreases by about a factor of 2 over the temperature range of 550 to $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
For example, the data for heat 5911 AW-anneal 1 indicate that aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ results in lower yield stress and a higher tensile stress than aging at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The anneals at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (3A and 2A) cause slight strength reductions.
The data for 5911 AW-anneal 1 indicate that aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ results in better ductility than aging at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

A comparison of the data for 5911 AW-anneals 1 and 3 indicate that the ductility of the materials receiving the anneal 3 is lower when aged and tested at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and higher at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The tensile properties of some of the materials were obtained at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after irradiation, and these results are given in Table 4.
A comparison of these data with those for the unirradiated specimens in Table 3 leads to several important observations:
The yield stress at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is higher for the irradiated specimens whereas the yield stress at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is equivalent for irradiated and unirradiated specimens.
The tensile stress is lower for the irradiated materials at both 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The ductility at both temperatures is reduced severely by irradiation, the reduction being much greater at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
At $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , for example, the unirradiated material shows a range of about 15 to $44\%$ , and the range is further extended by values as low as $5\%$ in the irradiated condition.
The results of tests on unirradiated and irradiated specimens are given in Tables 5 and 6, respectively.
Figure 3 shows that the rupture ductility varies from about 15 to $30\%$ for the unirradiated material at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Figure 4 compares the rupture lives of unirradiated and irradiated specimens at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The results on the present heat at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are inadequate to establish a stress-rupture curve, but the data are in reasonable agreement with those for the air melts.
5 as a function of the stress at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for irradiated and unirradiated materials.
The rupture strain at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is shown in Fig.
6 as a function of rupture life for unirradiated material.
The rupture strain varies from 10 to $50\%$ with most of the variation appearing to be independent of heat and annealing treatment.
One exception may be heat 6252 AC, which, after irradiation, has a greater rupture life.
At a stress level of 20,000 psi, the rupture life is reduced about two orders of magnitude by irradiation.

Although the tensile tests at a strain rate of $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ (12%/hr) indicated that the ductility was much lower at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the ductility appears to be independent of temperature at strain rates below about $0.1\% / \mathrm{hr}$ .
Figures 10, 11, 12, and 13 are direct photographs of the plotted data after the curves were drawn in manually.
Figures 10 and 11 show the results of tests on unirradiated and irradiated specimens, respectively, from heat 5911 AW-anneal 1 at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 40,000 psi.
The primary creep stage is very short, and the accumulated strain at the beginning of secondary creep was only about $0.1\%$ .
A Comparison of the Creep Rates of Irradiated and Unirradiated Hastelloy N at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Figures 12 and 13 show the results of tests at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 10,000 psi on unirradiated and irradiated specimens, respectively.
Since considerable data were obtained on heats 5911 AW and 5911 TH with heat treatment 1, the creep properties of these materials were examined in detail.
Further details of the creep behavior are given in Table 7.
At $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the behavior of the two lots of material was quite similar.
The times for rupture and $1\%$ strain for unirradiated and irradiated material at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are compared in Fig.
The curve for rupture of the irradiated material falls just short of the $1\%$ strain curve for the unirradiated specimens.
At $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ materials 5911 AW and 5911 TH have slightly different strength properties.
Figure 15 shows the results for heat 5911 AW-anneal 1.
At 8000 psi, the time to $1\%$ strain for the irradiated specimen agrees very well with that for the unirradiated material.
At 10,000 psi, the time to $1\%$ strain for the irradiated material is slightly less than that for the unirradiated specimen.
The data for heat 5911 TH-anneal 1 are plotted in Fig.
The time to $1\%$ strain for the irradiated material is much shorter than that for the unirradiated material at high stresses, but the two nearly converge at lower stresses.
16 concerning the location of the curve for the time to $1\%$ strain.
In an effort to determine the effects of strain rate on the rupture ductility at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , several specimens were tested in the unaged condition at various strain rates.
The results of these tests are given in Table 8.
The tensile results from Tables 3 and 8 and the creep results from Table 5 were used to obtain the plot of ductility versus strain rate in Fig.
At $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the ductility decreases with decreasing

At high strain rates, the ductility is lower at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and decreases with decreasing strain rate.
A minimum is reached at a strain rate of about $10\% / \mathrm{hr}$ and an increase in ductility occurs as the strain rate is decreased further.
Our metallographic studies were confined to the unirradiated tensile specimens that were aged and tested at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a strain rate of $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ .
The failures were all intergranular at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
At $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ extensive intergranular cracking occurred, and the failures were predominantly intergranular even though some areas failed by transgranular shear.
The microstructure of a specimen from heat 5911 AW-anneal 1 after aging and testing at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is shown in Fig.
The grain size is relatively coarse with stringers, probably of the $\mathrm{M}_{6}\mathrm{C}$ type.

The coarse microstructure of heat 5911 AW-anneal 3A is shown in Fig.
These were not visible in the specimen aged at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Small amounts of the same product were visible in the other heat 5911 AW specimens that were aged at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Heat 6252 AC was not homogenized adequately by any of the heat treatments used.
This material was received as a section cut from an as-cast 12-in.-diam billet.
Figure 22 shows the microstructure after anneal 2 and aging and testing at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Figure 23 shows that the working of treatments $2 + 3$ improved the homogeneity but not sufficiently to yield a typical microstructure.
Figure 24 shows that the higher temperature encountered in anneal 2A increased the grain size, but only increased the degree of segregation.

The dramatic decrease in tensile ductility with increasing test temperature is a characteristic of this alloy.<sup>4</sup> However, the exact mechanism of the ductility minimum is not understood.
17 shows that the rupture strain at high strain rates is greater at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and at low strain rates the converse is true.
The changes in tensile properties as a result of varying the anneal and the aging treatment (650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) are relatively small for such a complex alloy.
Although the results obtained on heat 6252 AC generally agreed with those for the other materials, several tests exhibited low ductility.
22-24 demonstrate the inhomogeneous nature of heat 6252 AC.
At $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the yield stress was increased and the tensile stress was decreased.
At $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the yield stress was unaffected and the tensile stress was reduced.
The stress-rupture life was reduced, the effect being greater at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The minimum creep rate was unaffected at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ but was increased at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The rupture strain was lowered in both tensile and creep-rupture tests, with the minimum strain occurring at a strain rate of 1 to $10\% / \mathrm{hr}$ .

This effect has been correlated with the thermal neutron exposure and more specifically with the helium that is formed by the $^{10}\mathrm{B}(n,\alpha)$ transmutation.[11] The boron, because of its size and low solubility, is initially segregated at the grain boundaries.[12] The recoil range of the $\alpha$ -particle produced by transmutation is $2\mu$ (ref.
13) so most of the helium will lie in the proximity of the grain boundaries.

Although several questions have been raised about some of the assumptions on which this equation is based, $^{15}$ the factors being considered seem quite reasonable and the equation is probably valid for qualitative arguments.

The increase in the yield stress at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after irradiating at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was not anticipated on the basis of previous work.[16] However, the previous work involved air-melted materials irradiated at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and a direct comparison cannot be made with the results of the present study.

Since $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is only approximately 0.55 of the absolute melting point of the alloy, there is also a possibility that some displacement defects such as dislocation loops may be present.
At $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the yield stress is unaffected.
The tensile stresses at both 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are reduced by irradiation.
effect is greater at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is probably due to the greater atomic mobility at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

For example, studies by Bloom and Stiegler<sup>17</sup> on type 304 stainless steel have failed to disclose any bubbles in material irradiated at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but have shown that the same material irradiated at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ contains bubbles up to 700 A in diameter.
As the strain rate is decreased, the conditions become more favorable for grain-boundary deformation<sup>18</sup> and the ductility of the irradiated material decreases rapidly.

The minimum fracture strain observed in a creep test with a creep rate of 1 to $10\% / \text{hr}$ is likely due to the worst combination of two factors: (1) low enough stress that the ratio of grain boundary to bulk deformation is fairly high and (2) high enough stress to cause many of the helium bubbles to grow.
The higher minimum creep rate of the irradiated material at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is difficult to explain, particularly since the rates converge at low stresses.
The specimens were irradiated to a thermal dose of $2.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² at temperatures of 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
At $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the yield stress was increased and the tensile stress was decreased.
At $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the yield stress was unaffected and the tensile stress was reduced.
The creep-rupture life was reduced, the effect being greater at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ than at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The minimum creep rate was unaffected at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ but was increased at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The rupture strain was lowered in both tensile and creep-rupture tests, with the minimum occurring at a strain rate of 1 to $10\% / \mathrm{hr}$ .
